strands.title: Migration Sequencing & Phasing
strands.content: A three-phase extraction strategy has been established for the 6-month window: Phase 1 (weeks 1-6) targets Authentication as the initial service boundary due to its minimal business logic, natural seam within the monolith, and unblocking effect on downstream services. Phase 2 (weeks 7-12) extracts Payment Processing with strict PCI-DSS compliance requirements—implemented as a thin processor wrapper that never handles raw card data, keeping audit scope minimal and enabling stateless design. Phase 3 (weeks 13-18) addresses Inventory Management, characterized by higher complexity (reservations, stock levels, warehouse locations) and requiring async event patterns with compensating transactions. Order Fulfillment & Orchestration deliberately remains in the monolith as the system's orchestrator—calling extracted services—reducing scope and keeping a steady command center. The monolith is expected to shrink from 500K to ~250K LOC by month 6. This sequencing prioritizes psychological and operational wins (auth first = boring, reliable) before tackling harder distributed system problems (inventory = eventual consistency, saga patterns). The strategy is conservative—it leaves order fulfillment monolithic, avoiding temptation to extract too much at once, which is a stated 'killer mistake.'

key_claims.text: Authentication service should be extracted first (weeks 1-6) because it has minimal business logic, natural seams with the monolith, and unblocks parallel development.
Payment Processing service must implement PCI-DSS compliance by wrapping a processor (Stripe/Square) and never touching raw card data directly.
Inventory Management extraction (weeks 13-18) requires async event patterns and compensating transactions, making it higher-risk than auth or payments.
By month 6, realistic outcome is auth and payment services in production, inventory 60% extracted, monolith reduced to 250K LOC, order fulfillment still monolithic.

strands.title: Infrastructure & Operational Foundation
strands.content: A concurrent infrastructure workstream is required in weeks 1-2: Docker/Kubernetes or ECS deployment setup, CI/CD pipelines for microservices (non-negotiable), centralized logging (ELK or Cloud Logging), and shared libraries for circuit breakers, logging, and monitoring. This foundation is prerequisite to service extraction and is deliberately front-loaded. Team structure proposed: 4-6 developers per service, 1-2 dedicated DBA/infrastructure, QA shifted left with service teams owning integration tests. Critical operational gap identified: distributed systems debugging requires investment in observability—logs alone are insufficient. Database migration strategy identified as a 2x estimation underrun risk (always takes longer than projected). Each extracted service must have its own database; shared databases are explicitly flagged as a 'killer mistake' to avoid, even if it means initial data duplication.

key_claims.text: Centralized logging (ELK or Cloud Logging) must be set up in weeks 1-2 before service extraction begins.
Each extracted microservice requires its own dedicated database; shared databases across services are an anti-pattern even if it creates duplication.
Database migration strategy historically takes 2x longer than estimated and is a killer risk.
Team structure should include 1-2 dedicated DBA/infrastructure personnel for 6-month migration.

strands.title: Technical Debt & Architectural Constraints
strands.content: The monolith is 500K LOC Java running on Tomcat. Auth service extraction specifically requires investigation into current session mechanism (session-based vs. token-based). If session-based, 2 weeks of compatibility work needed to add auth proxy layer translating old cookies to new JWTs, buying time for gradual rollout. Payment service implementation depends on existing processor integration (Stripe/Square assumed but not confirmed). Inventory service requires new infrastructure (Kafka/RabbitMQ for async events) and row-level locking strategy for stock counts. A fallback mechanism is critical: feature flags enabling instant rollback to monolith for each service call, reducing blast radius of failures. Network latency between services requires aggressive caching and circuit breakers on all inter-service calls with explicit timeouts. Database consistency patterns differ by service: payments require event sourcing for critical paths; inventory accepts eventual consistency. The approach deliberately avoids over-engineering distributed sagas in Phase 1, using synchronous HTTP calls with timeouts instead.

key_claims.text: Auth service must investigate current session mechanism; if session-based, 2 weeks of proxy compatibility work required.
Payment service should implement JWT-based stateless design with Redis blacklisting for logout.
Inventory service in Phase 1 should use synchronous HTTP calls with timeouts, not distributed sagas, to avoid over-engineering.
Feature flags for all inter-service calls enable instant fallback to monolith, reducing failure blast radius.

strands.title: Regulatory & Risk Management
strands.content: PCI-DSS compliance is the hard constraint driving payment service architecture. By implementing a thin wrapper around an external processor, the payment service reduces PCI audit scope to the minimum surface area (only processor integration, not card handling). This is a forcing function that produces clean separation. Risk mitigation table identifies four key risks: regression in monolith during extraction (mitigated by feature flags and instant fallback), network latency between services (mitigated by aggressive caching and circuit breakers), database consistency across services (mitigated by event sourcing for payments, eventual consistency for inventory), and team coordination overhead (mitigated by written OpenAPI contracts, spec-first development). Fallback strategy is critical: if payment service goes down, system should fail gracefully (don't charge, notify user), not propagate failure upstream. The migration is deliberately conservative—it avoids extracting too much at once, which is identified as a common 'killer mistake.'

key_claims.text: PCI-DSS compliance is achieved by implementing payment service as thin processor wrapper, keeping card data at external processor, never in-house.
Network latency between services must be mitigated by aggressive caching and circuit breakers with explicit timeouts on all calls.
Payment service failures should fail gracefully (don't charge, notify user) rather than propagate upstream.
Team coordination overhead is managed by requiring written OpenAPI specs before implementation ('no let's just call this API conversations').

strands.title: Realistic 6-Month Outcome & Expectations
strands.content: The 6-month window is positioned as a first-increment outcome, not final completion. Expected results: Auth service in production (reliable, boring); Payment service in production (PCI-compliant, minimal scope); Inventory service partially extracted (~60% of functionality); Monolith reduced from 500K to 250K LOC; Order fulfillment still monolithic (expected, not a failure). Inter-service call patterns are emerging but not yet optimized. This is explicitly framed as a 'good' outcome that proves the pattern, reduces risk, and leaves 12 months for finishing remaining work. The strategy acknowledges that inventory extraction (Phase 3) will likely not complete within 6 months due to complexity. Time budget risks are identified: database migration takes 2x longer than estimated; operational monitoring setup is under-budgeted. This framing manages expectations upward, avoiding the team's feeling of failure when order fulfillment remains monolithic.

key_claims.text: By month 6, realistic outcome is auth + payment in production, inventory 60% extracted, monolith at 250K LOC, order fulfillment monolithic.
The 6-month outcome is explicitly a first increment; 12 more months remain for finishing extracted services.
Keeping order fulfillment monolithic for 6 months is an intentional design choice, not a failure.
